Guzmán González Why I'm Applying Case StudiesImpact ExperienceJobs Impact WitnessesWitnesses
Change & Transformation Lead — Why I'm Applying

The work your team is set up to do — embedding inside agencies, diagnosing what is actually blocking modernisation, and building the conditions for change to last — is precisely what I have spent the last ten years doing.

Not as a consultant who delivers recommendations and leaves, but as someone who stays until the system can run without me.

For a team being built from the ground up, I can also act as a coach and internal leader — gradually widening the scope and complexity of problems our teammates or agency teams can take on independently, so the team's collective capability grows with every engagement.

I have built programs from first principles before. I know what it takes to earn trust inside complex institutions fast, to work across design, product, and engineering teams without sitting above any of them, and to leave agencies more capable than when I arrived. I am also writing a book — half philosophy, half tactical playbook — called Futures Worth Following, about leading change with integrity in conditions of growing complexity and uncertainty.

Singapore is home and a place we care about. Both my sons were born here and the public sector transformation work this team is doing matters to me directly — because when change doesn't stick inside a government agency, better futures don't materialise and citizens bear the cost.

Case Studies
Change Management
How to Drive Change Without a Blueprint
Dentsu iProspect APAC · Singapore · 2018–2019

The Challenge

A 400-person APAC digital agency in Singapore with nine siloed teams, high churn, margins under pressure. Brought in from London to modernise ways of working. Cynicism was high after multiple failed change initiatives.

The Diagnosis

The voiced problem was process inefficiency. The real problem was the incentive systems, which pushed people towards behaviours causing every downstream issue. No redesign would survive until that changed.

The Approach

Secured buy-in to redesign incentives first, then co-designed DDO — anchored in: we protect each other's time. Shifted from activity measurement to performance, outcome and behaviour indicators. Built to run without me.

Lasting Outcomes

−50% churn in 6 months · +33% pitch success · 0 unprofitable clients · ~1 day/wk saved per employee

DDO still in use after I left. Several function heads took it to their next organisations.

Impact Witness

"He not only delivers in spades but is widely acclaimed as a man who will do anything to help on any project."

Jack Cantwell — Global Client Partner, Dentsu Global
How I would do it today with AI

The diagnosis took interviews, documents, workshops, then processing it all to find the real pattern. With AI, I could have fed in contracts, engagement data, and delivery records and surfaced the contradictions between stated workflows and actual behaviour in days. Processing it all faster. Same conclusion, far more evidence, fraction of the time.

The approach would have looked different too. Instead of a fragmented picture across nine teams, AI could have powered a single source of truth visible to everyone, updated automatically. I could have linked Finance, and teams' APIs directly with client projects and create a historic of profitability, same as a live dashboard that reviews scope, time, and budget and other culture and change KPIs for each project creating alerts, insights and even recommendations when necessary — turning the whole approach into a consulting + SaaS.

The human work still takes the time it takes. But I could have done it in half the time to better results.

Real Innovation
Building Innovation Capacity from First Principles
Rainmaking / Engie Factory · Singapore · 2019–2020

The Challenge

Design and lead an open innovation program for Engie Factory — new sector, no methodology, six months, team of three. The Engie VPs had no P&L incentive to participate.

The Diagnosis

Incentive misalignment at VP level. I went to Engie's APAC CEO directly — proposing each unit tie revenue targets to new business lines. VP engagement followed.

The Approach

Pilot ID (2 months): Screen 1,000+ startups globally. Co-Pilot (4 months): Agile design sprints, pilots with measurable ROI. Five concurrent pilots. Adapted to remote mid-COVID. Methodology productised.

Lasting Outcomes

Built on a commercial model with skin in the game — profit only if we succeeded.

1,000+ startups screened · 5 concurrent pilots · SGD 4M revenue approved · 33% pilot success rate

Methodology adopted by Rainmaking for other clients and geographies — the approach outlasted the engagement.

Impact Witness

"I have been impressed by the quality of his work and the enthusiasm and structure with which complex problems were tackled and moved forward."

Quentin Vaquette — former Engie Factory MD
How I would have done it with AI

Scouting would have run on agents — one per funnel, screening startups at volume while a separate layer synthesised internal intelligence with industry foresight to map opportunities in real time. Weeks of work becomes a continuously updated shortlist with reasoning attached.

For Co-Pilot, AI changes the scale question. Business model simulation, live pilot instrumentation, and real-time A/B testing would have let us run one pilot per VP workstream — nine concurrent, partially automated.

The bigger shift: instead of a methodology productised after the fact, an AI-driven Open Innovation Operating System from day one — one platform, fully instrumented, repeatable across clients and geographies without rebuilding each time.

Change by Design
Scaling Change Inside a Global Product Organisation
Apple · Singapore · 2020–2023

The Challenge

Build a new distributed program from scratch — 20 people in 10 countries to nearly 400 in 40 countries, 12 Apple products, 4 business lines, under three years. No operating model, no ways of working, no measurement system. Across 14 highly regulated markets.

The Diagnosis

A fast-scaling operation cannot be designed sequentially. The right call: coherence over perfection. Build the minimum shared system people could trust, so decisions could propagate without escalation.

The Approach

Designed the full operating model, governance, and measurement framework — embedded across all functions. Built a custom Airtable system from scratch, taught it to 400 people in 40 countries. Playbooks across 6 functions. 300+ training and change sessions.

Lasting Outcomes

20→350 people in 3 years · 40 countries · 10× budget expansion · 85–110% KPIs sustained

The operating model held throughout. The infrastructure did not become the bottleneck as the program scaled.

Impact Witness

"A mastermind of change management. I've rarely seen a business so thoroughly improved in such a short time. I cannot think of a single person who was not impressed by the quality and the impact of his work."

Jose Campon — WPP China CEO · direct manager at Apple
How I would do it today with AI

The operating model would have been built as a modular AI-driven platform from day one — structured around the actual scaling logic: countries × business lines × products. Every new combination inherits the existing framework. No rebuilding as you grow.

Onboarding and knowledge automated and adaptive — specific to team, country, and function. Not replacing the human-to-human experience but raising the quality of it.

The bigger shift is how the work itself would have been designed. Not AI retrofitted onto existing workflows — but ways of working redesigned from scratch as human-AI workflows, bespoke to each function and context. AI handling the operational load, humans owning judgment and relationships. A different operating model, not just a faster one.

The platform wouldn't just process work — it would surface proactive recommendations, flag drift, and synthesise signals across 40 countries that no small team could monitor manually.

The result: instead of operational work consuming everything, the team would have had the capacity to operate at a fundamentally higher level.

The Challenge

A 400-person APAC digital agency in Singapore with nine siloed teams, high churn, margins under pressure, and a regional remit. Brought in from London to modernise ways of working. Cynicism was high after multiple failed change initiatives.

The Diagnosis

The voiced problem was process inefficiency. But people's behaviour revealed the real problem was the incentive systems and success measurement, which pushed people towards behaviours causing every downstream issue. No redesign would survive until that changed.

The Approach

A lot of the work was dedicated to securing buy-in to redesign incentives and success metrics first. We co-designed DDO — a ways-of-working system — anchored in a shared belief: we protect each other's time. Tested with a small team, adjusted, scaled. Shifted from activity measurement to a mix of performance, outcome and behaviour indicators. Built feedback loops, empowered champions, so it could run without me.

Lasting Outcomes

−50% employee churn in 6 months · +33% pitch success rate · 0 unprofitable clients · ~1 day/wk saved per employee

DDO still in use after I left. Several function heads took it to their next organisations.

Impact Witness

"He not only delivers in spades but is widely acclaimed as a man who will do anything to help on any project if it will help you or the business."

Jack Cantwell — Global Client Partner, Dentsu Global
How I would do it today with AI

The diagnosis took interviews, documents, workshops, then processing it all to find the real pattern. With AI, I could have fed in contracts, engagement data, and delivery records and surfaced the contradictions between stated workflows and actual behaviour in days. Processing it all faster. Same conclusion, far more evidence, fraction of the time.

The approach would have looked different too. Instead of a fragmented picture across nine teams, AI could have powered a single source of truth visible to everyone, updated automatically. I could have linked Finance, and teams' APIs directly with client projects and create a historic of profitability, same as a live dashboard that reviews scope, time, and budget and other culture and change KPIs for each project creating alerts, insights and even recommendations when necessary — turning the whole approach into a consulting + SaaS.

The human work still takes the time it takes. But I could have done it in half the time to better results.

The Challenge

Design and lead an open innovation program for Engie Factory in Singapore — new sector, no methodology, six months, a team of three. The deeper problem: the Engie VPs we needed had no P&L incentive to participate. Good methodology alone would not move them.

The Diagnosis

The real problem was incentive misalignment at VP level. Without P&L skin in the game, participation was irrational. I paused the design work and went to Engie's APAC CEO directly — proposing that each business unit tie a percentage of next year's revenue to a product or service line that didn't exist the year before. This reframed the lab from optional to strategic. VP engagement followed.

The Approach

Pilot ID (2 months): Map verticals, identify opportunity spaces, screen 1,000+ startups globally for technology maturity and fit.

Co-Pilot (4 months): Select startups, run agile design sprints, build and test pilots with measurable ROI indicators for both sides. Five concurrent pilots. Program adapted to remote delivery mid-COVID. Methodology productised for replication across other clients without us.

Lasting Outcomes

Built on a commercial model with skin in the game — profit only if we succeeded.

1,000+ startups screened · 5 concurrent pilots · SGD 4M revenue approved · 33% pilot success rate

Pilot ID and Co-Pilot adopted by Rainmaking for other clients and geographies — the methodology outlasted the engagement.

Impact Witness

"I have been impressed by the quality of his work and the enthusiasm and structure with which complex problems were tackled and moved forward."

Quentin Vaquette — former Engie Factory MD
How I would have done it with AI

Scouting would have run on agents — one per funnel, screening startups at volume while a separate layer synthesised internal intelligence with industry foresight to map opportunities in real time. Weeks of work becomes a continuously updated shortlist with reasoning attached.

For Co-Pilot, AI changes the scale question. Business model simulation, live pilot instrumentation, and real-time A/B testing would have let us run one pilot per VP workstream — nine concurrent, partially automated.

The bigger shift: instead of a methodology productised after the fact, an AI-driven Open Innovation Operating System from day one — one platform, fully instrumented, repeatable across clients and geographies without rebuilding each time.

The Challenge

Build a new distributed program from scratch during the pandemic — 20 people in 10 countries to nearly 400 people in 40 countries, serving 12 Apple products and 4 business lines, in under three years. No operating model, no ways of working, no measurement system, no internal tech. Across 14 highly regulated markets, navigating compliance, data sensitivity, and local institutional requirements as a constant.

The Diagnosis

A fast-scaling operation with Apple's quality standards cannot be designed sequentially — by the time a process is defined, the context has already changed. The right call: coherence over perfection. Build the minimum shared system people could trust, so decisions could propagate without escalation.

The Approach

Designed the full operating model, governance, and measurement framework — embedded across all functions, not delivered as a consulting output. Built a custom project management system in Airtable from scratch, then taught it to 400 people across 40 countries. Playbooks across 6 functions. 300+ training and change sessions. Embedded user-centric practices into delivery from the start, not as retrofit.

Lasting Outcomes

20→350 people in 3 years · 40 countries serviced · 10× budget expansion · 85–110% KPIs sustained

The operating model and measurement framework held throughout. The infrastructure did not become the bottleneck as the program scaled.

Impact Witness

"A mastermind of change management. I've rarely seen a business so thoroughly improved in such a short time. I cannot think of a single person who was not impressed by the quality and the impact of his work."

Jose Campon — WPP China CEO · direct manager at Apple
How I would do it today with AI

The operating model would have been built as a modular AI-driven platform from day one — structured around the actual scaling logic: countries × business lines × products. Every new combination inherits the existing framework. No rebuilding as you grow.

Onboarding and knowledge automated and adaptive — specific to team, country, and function. Not replacing the human-to-human experience but raising the quality of it.

The bigger shift is how the work itself would have been designed. Not AI retrofitted onto existing workflows — but ways of working redesigned from scratch as human-AI workflows, bespoke to each function and context. AI handling the operational load, humans owning judgment and relationships. A different operating model, not just a faster one.

The platform wouldn't just process work — it would surface proactive recommendations, flag drift, and synthesise signals across 40 countries that no small team could monitor manually.

The result: instead of operational work consuming everything, the team would have had the capacity to operate at a fundamentally higher level.

Experience

  • Co-designing and implementing new ways-of-working, AI adoption, digital transformation, product thinking, user-centric redesign, operational fairness-coherence, and facilitating cross-functional workshops and stakeholder alignment sessions.
  • Teaching a mix of Systems Thinking, Organizational Design, Behavioral Sciences, Project Management & Transformation.
  • Example: embedded change lead for form-three (product design) to help a global NGO with the above as they tripled size.

Impact Witnesses

Three patterns emerge independently across 20 professional references spanning 10+ years — from CEOs and direct reports. 4 countries · 6 industries · 5 organisations · zero hedging or qualification in any reference.

Enters unfamiliar industries — energy, big tech, government — and reads the real problem within weeks. Referees independently express surprise at the speed. This pattern appears across every organisation and every seniority level in the dataset.

But don't just take it from me +

"It became apparent quite quickly that he was the go-to-guy to understand how things actually got done. It shocked me when I learned he had only been in for 6 months."

Matthew Schone — former CPO, Dentsu APAC

"He was able to parachute into a very complex project in a totally new industry and immediately structure approaches to solving our key challenges."

Colin Allison — Program Lead, Rainmaking

Not "delivered projects" — lasting. Processes introduced at Dentsu are still in use years after departure. Programs built at Rainmaking scaled to other clients. Systems designed at Apple supported a 10× budget expansion. The work survived the person leaving.

But don't just take it from me +

"To this day there are processes and improvements he introduced that are still impactful on how we work at iProspect/Dentsu."

Nader Ezzat — Strategic Project Management Expert, Dentsu

"He was able to introduce process improvements that have since elevated and strengthened the way that we run our various projects."

Rachel Tan — Strategic Operator, Rainmaking

The highest-frequency signal. Not a side effect of competence but described as a primary capability — the mechanism through which results are produced. Direct reports credit specific growth. Peers describe feeling supported. Executives note cultural uplift.

But don't just take it from me +

Samuel Hall — CEO, Rainmaking

"He truly cares about his team and spends invaluable time teaching and growing team members around him."
